attributes.stem:
A 10-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with a two-month history of recurrent epistaxis. He has also noticed more frequent unexplained bruising on his body. His grandmother has a bleeding disorder, but he cannot recall its name. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. During the examination, an episode of epistaxis occurs with visible bleeding from Little's area. Direct pressure applied to the septum for 10 minutes stops the bleeding completely. Investigations reveal:

  • Prolonged bleeding time
  • Prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT)
  • Normal prothrombin time (PT)
  • Normal platelet count
  • Haemoglobin and white cell count within normal range
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation and laboratory findings in this case are most consistent with von Willebrand disease. This is the most common inherited bleeding disorder, characterized by a deficiency in the quantity or quality of von Willebrand factor (vWF). The key features supporting this diagnosis are:

1. Recurrent epistaxis and easy bruising, which are common symptoms of von Willebrand disease.

2. Family history of a bleeding disorder, suggesting an inherited condition.

3. Prolonged bleeding time and aPTT, with normal PT and platelet count. This pattern is typical of von Willebrand disease, as vWF affects platelet adhesion (prolonging bleeding time) and stabilizes factor VIII (prolonging aPTT).

Factor X deficiency would result in prolongation of both PT and aPTT, as Factor X is involved in both the intrinsic and extrinsic coagulation pathways. This pattern is not seen in the given results.

Vitamin K deficiency primarily affects the vitamin K-dependent clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X), leading to a prolonged PT. The normal PT in this case makes vitamin K deficiency unlikely.

Immune thrombocytopenia is characterized by a low platelet count, which is not present in this case. Additionally, aPTT would typically be normal in immune thrombocytopenia.

Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia would typically present with abnormalities in the complete blood count, including anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and often an abnormal white cell count. The normal haemoglobin, white cell count, and platelet count make this diagnosis unlikely.

In conclusion, the combination of clinical presentation, family history, and laboratory findings strongly supports von Willebrand disease as the most likely diagnosis. Further testing, such as vWF antigen levels and factor VIII activity, would be necessary to confirm the diagnosis and determine the subtype of von Willebrand disease.
learningPoints: Von Willebrand disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder in children, characterized by prolonged bleeding time and aPTT with normal PT and platelet count.
Family history is an important clue in diagnosing inherited bleeding disorders like von Willebrand disease.
references: Kucik CJ, Clenney T. Management of epistaxis. Am Fam Physician. 2005 Jan 15;71(2):305-11. PMID: 15686301.
"What Is Hemophilia?". NHLBI. July 13, 2013. Archived from the original on 4 October 2016

Tunkel DE, Anne S, Payne SC, Ishman SL, Rosenfeld RM, Abramson PJ, Alikhaani JD, Benoit MM, Bercovitz RS, Brown MD, Chernobilsky B, Feldstein DA, Hackell JM, Holbrook EH, Holdsworth SM, Lin KW, Lind MM, Poetker DM, Riley CA, Schneider JS, Seidman MD, Vadlamudi V, Valdez TA, Nnacheta LC, Monjur TM. Clinical Practice Guideline: Nosebleed (Epistaxis). Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg. 2020 Jan;162(1_suppl):S1-S38. doi: 10.1177/0194599819890327. PMID: 31910111.

Sabih A, Babiker HM. Von Willebrand Disease. [Updated 2022 Jul 10]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459222/

Chatterjee, T., Philip, J., Nair, V., Mallhi, R. S., Sharma, H., Ganguly, P., & Biswas, A. K. (2015). Inherited Factor X (Stuart-Prower Factor) deficiency and its management. Medical journal, Armed Forces India, 71(Suppl 1), S184–S186. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mjafi.2014.01.007
